coredata.dc:title: On-off-on fluorescent carbon dots from waste tea: Their properties, antioxidant and selective detection of CrO4 2−, Fe3+, ascorbic acid and L-cysteine in real samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Kui
Qing, Weixia
Hu, Weiping
Lu, Minghua
Wang, Yong
Liu, Xiuhua
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we reported an economical plant-based hydrothermal method for one-pot green synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots (Tea-CDs) by using waste tea extract as a carbon source. The synthesized Tea-CDs were characterized by UV–visible, fluorescence, FT-IR, TEM, XPS and XRD. The Tea-CDs were found to remove hydroxyl and superoxide anion radical in vitro. In addition, the Tea-CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV-light (λex = 365 nm), and the fluorescence could be effectively quenched by CrO4 2− and Fe3+ ions. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ systems could be again easily recovered by ascorbic acid (AA) and L-cysteine (L-Cys). As an on-off-on fluorescent nano-sensor of the Tea-CDs, the sensitive detection of CrO4 2−, Fe3+, AA and L-Cys were all performed, showing that the good linear relationships between fluorescence intensity of Tea-CDs and concentration of all testing samples. Finally, the sensors successfully detected CrO4 2−, Fe3+, AA and L-Cys in commercially available real samples with satisfactory recovery ranges. The prepared sensors offer distinct advantages including low cost, simple handling, good sensitivity and high selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: On-off-on fluorescent nano-sensor
Carbon dots
Waste tea
Metal ions
Ascorbic acid
L-cysteine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Tea-CDs From the Waste Tea
Detection of CrO4 2− and Fe3+ With Tea-CDs
The Effect of pH, NaCl and UV Solution on Tea-CDs
Response of Tea-CDs to CrO4 2− and Fe3+
Tea-CDs for the Detection of CrO4 2− and Fe3+
Detection of AA and L-Cys with Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern, (b) FT-IR spectrum, (c) TEM image and (d) fluorescence delay time of Tea-CDs.
(a) Survey, (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s XPS spectra of Tea-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra (λex = 350 nm) of Tea-CDs in aqueous solution; inset: photographs of Tea-CDs solution under daylight and 365 nm UV light. (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of Tea-CD aqueous solution under the different excitation wavelength ranging from 290 to 400 nm.
Scavenging ability of the Tea-CDs against hydroxyl radical (a) and superoxide radical (b).

Fluorescence emission spectra of Tea-CDs solution (5 μg/mL) at different concentrations of CrO4 2− (a) and Fe3+ (b). And their linear relationship between I0/I-1 and the concentrations of CrO4 2− (a inset) and Fe3+ (b inset).

Fluorescence emission spectra of Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ system at different concentrations of AA (a and c) and L-Cys (b and d). And their linear relationship between I0/I-1 and the concentrations of AA (inset of a and c) and L-Cys (inset of b and d).
Detection of CrO4 2− and Fe3+ with Tea-CDs.
Detection of AA with Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+.
Detection of L-Cys with Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+.

head.ce:title: On-off-on fluorescent carbon dots from waste tea: Their properties, antioxidant and selective detection of CrO4 2−, Fe3+, ascorbic acid and L-cysteine in real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental and Analytical Sciences, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental and Analytical Sciences
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Nanomedicine, School of Basic Medical Science, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Natural Medicine and Immune-Engineering of Henan Province, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Natural Medicine and Immune-Engineering of Henan Province

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: X. Liu. Key Laboratory of Natural Medicine and Immune-Engineering of Henan Province, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Natural Medicine and Immune-Engineering of Henan Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we reported an economical plant-based hydrothermal method for one-pot green synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots (Tea-CDs) by using waste tea extract as a carbon source. The synthesized Tea-CDs were characterized by UV–visible, fluorescence, FT-IR, TEM, XPS and XRD. The Tea-CDs were found to remove hydroxyl and superoxide anion radical in vitro. In addition, the Tea-CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV-light (λex = 365 nm), and the fluorescence could be effectively quenched by CrO4 2− and Fe3+ ions. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ systems could be again easily recovered by ascorbic acid (AA) and L-cysteine (L-Cys). As an on-off-on fluorescent nano-sensor of the Tea-CDs, the sensitive detection of CrO4 2−, Fe3+, AA and L-Cys were all performed, showing that the good linear relationships between fluorescence intensity of Tea-CDs and concentration of all testing samples. Finally, the sensors successfully detected CrO4 2−, Fe3+, AA and L-Cys in commercially available real samples with satisfactory recovery ranges. The prepared sensors offer distinct advantages including low cost, simple handling, good sensitivity and high selectivity.

The hydrothermal method based on cheaper waste tea for one-pot green synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots (Tea-CDs) was reported. And the prepared Tea-CDs was an on-off-on fluorescent nano-sensor, showing the sensitive detection of CrO4 2−, Fe3+, AA and L-Cys in real samples with satisfactory recovery ranges.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of the world economy, water pollution is one of the focus of the global problems as lots of industrial wastewater containing heavy metal ions have been discharged. Compared to the other toxic metal ions, Cr(VI) is greater hazardous to human health because of its high toxicity, carcinogenesis, teratogenicity and mutagenicity [1,2]. The maximum concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water regulated by the World Health Organization is 50 μg/L, but Cr(VI) concentration in the wastewater is far more than the allowable guideline [3]. In addition the excess Fe3+ ion in human body can induce serious physiological damages [4,5]. Thus, it is important to detect Cr(VI) and Fe3+ in drinking water and industrial wastewater.

Currently, carbon-based dots (CDs) have attracted much attention because of their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and easy preparation [6–8]. Based on these advantages, CDs have a good application prospect in fluorescent probes, photo-catalyst, drug delivery, bioimaging and drugs or metal ions analysis [9–13]. Some methods have been established for the preparation of carbon-dots, such as hydrothermal method, microwave method, ultrasonic method, and so on [14,15]. However, most carbon sources of the previously reported methods were expensive. So a cheap and sustainable source of raw materials should be explored for the preparation of carbon sources.

It is well known green tea is one of the most popular drinks worldwide, while a large number of tea stalks and dust are removed during different stages of processing, which not only causes great waste of raw material, but also environmental pollution. In order to develop and utilize the waste tea, here a new “on-off” fluorescent nano-sensor (Tea-CDs) based on waste tea was firstly established to quantitatively characterize CrO4 2− and Fe3+ ions. The funny fact was found that the fluorescence of both Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ systems could recover by AA and L-Cys, respectively. So both Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ systems again could act as “off-on” fluorescence sensors for the determination of AA and L-Cys. Compared to previously reported methods, we for the first time developed an economical and green hydrothermal method to prepare water-soluble Tea-CDs by using waste tea as a carbon source [16,17]. Furthermore, the prepared “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors are low cost with the characteristics of fast response, high sensitivity and selectivity [18].

The waste tea was bought from the tea line (Henan, Kaifeng). Ascorbic acid, L-cysteine, ethanediamine and all metal salts (including K2Cr2O7, FeCl3, BaCl2, NiSO4, BeSO4, FeSO4, MnSO4, Cr(NO)3, Sr(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Pb(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, LiNO3 and AgNO3) were bought from Aladdin Industrial Inc. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout all the experiment. The solutions of metal ions were prepared from their nitrate, sulfate, or chloride salts (see support information for details).

UV–vis spectrum was recorded on a TU-1900 spectrophotometer in the wavelength ranging from 220 to 700 nm (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co. Ltd.). Fluorescence spectroscopy was obtained with a Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer (JEOL Co., England). The morphologies were studied on JEM-2100 (JEOL Co., Japan). XRD patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu K radiation and 2θ range was 10–90°. The FT-IR spectrum was studied on a Thermo Nicolet Avatar 360 spectrometer in the wavenumber ranging from 500 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried on an Thermo ESCALAB 250XI photoelectron spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Tea-CDs From the Waste Tea
ce:section.ce:para: Tea-CDs was prepared by using hydrothermal treatment based on the waste tea. Specifically, the waste tea (1.5 g) was immersed in 30 mL of deionized water and boiled at 80 °C for 30 min. The obtained extract was then mixed with 10 μL ethanediamine and placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 150 °C for 6 h. The Teflon-lined autoclave was allowed to cool down naturally and centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles. Finally, the brown solution was freeze-dried and reserved.

Hydroxyl radical was determined using the Fenton reaction. Briefly, 1 mL of different concentrations (0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5 mg/mL) of Tea-CDs added FeSO4 (1 mL, 0.01 mol/L), NaAc-HAc buffer solution (5 mL), 0.12% H2O2 (1 mL), methyl violet (1 mL, 0.002 mol/L) and deionized water (1 mL) in order. After incubated for 1 h at room temperature, the absorbance of the mixture was detected at 600 nm. The scavenging of hydroxyl radical was calculated by the following Eq. (1):
(1)
S % =
A s
−
A 0
/
A −
A 0
× 100
where A s is the absorbance of the test sample; A 0 is the absorbance of the control (reaction solution without sample) and A is the absorbance of the control (reaction solution without FeSO4).

The scavenging of superoxide radical was determined with the autoxidation of pyrogallol method [19]. Briefly, 0.2 mL of different concentrations (0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.2 mg/mL) of Tea-CDs added Tris-HCl buffer (5.7 mL, pH 8.2) and pyrogallol (0.1 mL). After incubated for 20 min at room temperature, the absorbance of the mixture was detected at 320 nm. The scavenging of superoxide radical was calculated by the following Eq. (2):
(2)
S % =
A 0
−
A s
/
A 0
× 100
where A 0 is the absorbance of the control (reaction solution without sample) and A s is the absorbance of the test sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CrO4 2− and Fe3+ With Tea-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A series of different concentration of CrO4 2− and Fe3+ ions were respectively mixed with 1 mL of Tea-CDs solution (10 μg/mL). The final volume of the solution was added to 2 mL with deionized water and the concentrations of metal ions were in the range of 0.1–20 μg/mL. After incubation for 1 min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the solutions was recorded on a fluorescence spectroscopy at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. All experiments were repeated three times.

Both Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ systems were further used for detection of AA and L-Cys, respectively. A series of different concentrations of AA and L-Cys were added to Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ systems, respectively. After incubation for 1 min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the solutions was recorded on a fluorescence spectroscopy at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Same as the previous experiment, all experiments were repeated three times.

Tea-CDs was first respectively prepared via the thermal treatment of waste tea extract and the mixture of ethanediamine with waste tea extract at 150 °C. Two brown Tea-CDs solutions were obtained. Compared waste tea extract, Tea-CDs from the mixture of ethanediamine with waste tea extract exhibited bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. S1). So the effect of different volume of ethylenediamine on Tea-CDs fluorescence intensity was further investigated. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of Tea-CDs solution was the highest when 10 μL of ethylenediamine was added into extract (Fig. S2). Therefore, the follow-up experiments were carried out on Tea-CDs prepared by the mixture of ethanediamine (10 μL) with waste tea extract.

As shown in Fig. 1a, a broad diffraction peak around 23.76° was observed, which was consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of carbon based materials [20]. As shown in Fig. 1b, a very broad absorption bands at around 3361 cm−1 was attributed to OH and NH stretching vibration. The peak at 2926 cm−1 was attributed to CH stretching vibration. The peak at 1700 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching vibration, and the peak at 1649 cm−1 indicated the presence of CC stretching. Other peaks at 1452, 1363, and 1043 cm−1 indicated the presence of NH bending vibration and CN stretching vibration, respectively [21]. Fig. 1c showed the Tea-CDs was monodisperse and spherical and particle size was about 10 nm. The PL decay curve of Tea-CDs (Fig. 1d) was well-fitted by a following dual-exponential formula (3):
(3)
I
t
=
A 1
exp
− t /
т 1
+
A 2
exp
− t /
т 2
where т1 and т2 are the time constants of the two radiative decay channels; A 1 and A 2 are the corresponding amplitudes. According to the best fitting data, the lifetime of Tea-CDs is 13.51 ns.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the component and surface states of the Tea-CDs were characterized by XPS. Fig. 2a revealed that the obtained Tea-CDs mainly contained carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. Calculated from the XPS spectrum, the C: N: O atomic ratio in the Tea-CDs was 59.42: 7.81: 32.77. In detail, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) showed three peaks at 284.5, 285.7, 286.5 and 288.6 eV, which were attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO and CO/CN, respectively [22]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) showed three peaks at 398.3, 400.3 and 401.4 eV, which was ascribed to CNC, CN and NH, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) showed two peaks at 531.9 and 532.8 eV, which were ascribed to CO and COH/COC, respectively [23].

In UV–vis spectrum of Tea-CDs (Fig. 3 ), a sharp peak at about 270 nm and a broad peak at around 330 nm were observed. The peak at 270 and 330 nm might be attributed to π → π⁎ transitions of the CC bond, and n → π⁎ transitions of the CO bond, respectively [24]. And as shown in Fig. 3 inset, the Tea-CDs solution under daylight showed a brown color (left) while it showed a bright blue fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm wavelength (right). Simultaneously, the maximum emission wavelength shifted from 440 to 460 nm with increasing of the excitation wavelength from 290 to 400 nm. And the strongest emission intensity at 445 nm with an excitation of 350 nm was observed (Fig. 3b). The quantum yield of Tea-CDs was measured to be about 7.1% (specific details see supporting information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Effect of pH, NaCl and UV Solution on Tea-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S3a, the pH effect on the fluorescent intensity of Tea-CDs had been studied, which showed that the fluorescent intensity could remain stable when the pH value was not less than 5. Therefore, all experiments were carried out in deionized water without adjusting pH value of solution. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S3b, Tea-CDs also had high stability in high NaCl solution. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S3c, Tea-CDs showed exceptional stability and the fluorescent intensity still retained 87.7% of initial intensity under a 15 W 365 nm UV light for a period of 2 h.

The scavenging rate of hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical are important indicators of antioxidant capacity. As shown in Fig. 4 , the scavenging ability of Tea-CDs against both hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical increased with increasing Tea-CDs concentration. The IC50 values of hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical were respectively 80 and 24.2 μg/mL, which showed that the scavenging capability of Tea-CDs against either hydroxyl radical or superoxide radical was conspicuous.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of Tea-CDs to CrO4 2− and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The appropriate concentration of Tea-CDs was determined. After the addition of same concentration of CrO4 2− to series of Tea-CDs solution, the results showed that the fluorescence quenching was the best when Tea-CDs concentration was 5 μg/mL. Therefore, the concentration of Tea-CDs was 5 μg/mL in subsequent experiment. In order to study the selectivity of Tea-CDs for the determination of metal ions, totally 18 kinds of ions including CrO4 2−, Cr3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Be2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Li+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+ were detected. After the addition of 20 μg/mL of various ions to Tea-CDs solution (5 μg/mL), the fluorescent intensity at 445 nm was recorded (Fig. S4a). The results showed that only CrO4 2− and Fe3+ ions lead to the sharp decrease of I 445. There were several possible reasons as follows. For the decrease by CrO4 2−, as shown in Fig. S5, the absorption bands of CrO4 2− covered the excitation and emission bands of Tea-CDs resulted in the inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence quenching of Tea-CDs [25,26]. For that by Fe3+, there were lots of hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of Tea-CDs, which interacted with Fe3+, resulting in a nonradiative electron transfer from the excited state of Tea-CDs [27]. In addition, the effects of incubation time on the fluorescent intensity of Tea-CDs in the presence of CrO4 2− and Fe3+ were also studied. After the addition of 5 μg/mL of CrO4 2− and Fe3+ to Tea-CDs solution (5 μg/mL), respectively, the I 445 decreased greatly and did not change after 1 min (Fig. S4b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tea-CDs for the Detection of CrO4 2− and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of Tea-CDs for both CrO4 2− and Fe3+ was evaluated by adding different concentrations of CrO4 2− and Fe3+ into Tea-CDs solution, and the corresponding fluorescence response was recorded. With increasing of CrO4 2− and Fe3+ concentration (0.1–25 μg/mL), the fluorescence intensity of Tea-CDs gradually decreases, while the shape and position of the fluorescence emission peak did not change (Fig. 5 ). A plot of I0/I-1 against the CrO4 2− concentration was obtained, and a good linearity was observed in the range of 1.9–115.4 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9975 (Fig. 5a inset). For Fe3+, a good linearity was also observed in the range of 53.5–267.9 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9917 (Fig. 5b inset). The lowest detection concentrations (LOD) of CrO4 2− and Fe3+ were about 0.81 and 0.15 μM, respectively (see support information for details). So Tea-CDs can potentially be developed to be a sensitive sensor for the detection of CrO4 2− and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA and L-Cys with Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: It was reported that oxidation and reduction reaction between AA and Cr(VI) was carried out under mild conditions [21,26]. So the effects of some reductive compounds such as AA, L-Cys and glutathione on Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ fluorescence recovery were studied, and the results showed that both AA and L-Cys could restore fluorescence of Tea-CDs. The possible reasons were as follows. CrO4 2− ion could be quickly reduced to Cr3+ by AA and L-Cys, resulting in the elimination of IFE and recovery of Tea-CDs fluorescence. While compared to combining hydroxyls, Fe3+ ion was much easier to be reduced by AA and L-Cys, thus recovery of Tea-CDs fluorescence. Then, the effects of incubation time on the fluorescent intensity of Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ systems in the presence of AA and L-Cys were also studied. After the addition AA and L-Cys to Tea-CDs solution, respectively, the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm was no longer change until 1 min later. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence of both Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ systems was enhanced gradually with increasing concentrations of AA and L-Cys. The percentage of recovery of AA and L-Cys for Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− was 78.07% and 70.91%, respectively. And the percentage of recovery of AA and L-Cys for Tea-CDs-Fe3+ was 55.32 and 60.23, respectively. Meanwhile, good linear relationships of I/I0-1 and AA (L-Cys) concentration were observed in Fig. 6 insets. For Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− system, LOD of AA and L-Cys were about 87.02 and 8.785 μM, respectively. For Tea-CDs-Fe3+ system, LOD of AA and L-Cys were about 19.78 and 153.5 μM, respectively. So both Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− and Tea-CDs-Fe3+ systems can potentially be developed to be sensitive sensors for the detection of AA and L-Cys.

In order to check the performance of the prepared fluorescent nano-sensors, tap water, VC effervescent tablets and human urine samples were chosen for the determination of metal ions, AA and L-Cys, respectively [28]. The standard addition method was employed for the calculation recovery, and the samples were spiked with different known concentrations of metal ions, AA and L-Cys, respectively. The detected recovery rates of the prepared fluorescent sensors ranged from 98.3% to 102.3% (Tables 1a, 1b and 1c
). These results indicated that our prepared sensors were reliable, feasible and sensitive to detect metal ions, AA and L-Cys in water and beverages.

In summary, we developed an economical and green hydrothermal method to prepare Tea-CDs by using waste tea. The Tea-CDs were effective in removing hydroxyl and superoxide anion radical in vitro. Besides, as an on-off sensor, Tea-CDs can be sensitive to detect CrO4 2− and Fe3+ ions. However, as an off-on sensor, Tea-CDs-CrO4 2− (Tea-CDs-Fe3+) again can be sensitive to detect AA and L-Cys. Compared with some reported results (Table S1), the present Tea-CDs sensors showed more economical and sensitive. So our prepared Tea-CDs have potential application in the environmental and analytical chemistry fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in University of Henan Province in China (No. 17HASTIT003), and Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province in China (No. 162300410043 and 182300410235).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.01.066.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chromium in drinking water: sources, metabolism, and cancer risks
The inner filter effect of Cr(VI) on Tb-doped layered rare earth hydroxychlorides: new fluorescent adsorbents for the simple detection of Cr(VI)
Iron and cancer: more ore to be mined
On-off-on fluorescent nanosensor for Fe3+ detection and cancer/normal cell differentiation via silicon-doped carbon quantum dots

Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Highly efficient red-emitting carbon dots with gram-scale yield for bioimaging
Preparation of gold-carbon dots and ratiometric fluorescence cellular imaging
Novel fluorescence resonance energy transfer optical sensors for vitamin B12 detection using thermally reduced carbon dots
The selectivity of the carboxylate groups terminated carbon dots switched by buffer solutions for the detection of multi-metal ions
Design of Janus-like PMMA-PEG-FA grafted fluorescent carbon dots and their nanoassemblies for leakage-free tumor theranostic application
Synthesis and characterization of high efficient photoluminescent sunlight driven photocatalyst of N-Carbon Quantum Dots
One-step microwave assisted polyol synthesis of green luminescent carbon dots as optical nanoprobes
Upconversion and downconversion fluorescent graphene quantum dots: ultrasonic preparation and photocatalysis
Sustainable carbon-dots: recent advances in green carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging
Full color emitting fluorescent carbon material as reversible pH sensor with multicolor live cell imaging

Preparation of bioactive polysaccharide nanoparticles with enhanced radical scavenging activity and antimicrobial activity
One-pot green synthesis of carbon dots by using Saccharum officinarum juice for fluorescent imaging of bacteria (Escherichia coli) and yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) cells
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots for chemical sensing
Dual functional carbonaceous nanodots exist in a cup of tea

Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
On-Off-On fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
An “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot
Solid phase synthesis of nitrogen and phosphor co-doped carbon quantum dots for sensing Fe3+ and the enhanced photocatalytic degradation of dyes
Multicolour fluorescent carbon nanoparticle probes for live cell imaging and dual palladium and mercury sensors
